Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a consistent study routine. I'd listen to lectures and podcasts whenever I could fit them in—during commutes, while cooking, that kind of thing. With nine online courses going on, I just couldn't be organized about it. I'd kind of drift between whatever felt most urgent or whatever caught my attention that day. Some weeks I'd put in a lot of hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely not regular. I'd cram when deadlines were approaching but otherwise just kind of let things slide. Working with limited resources means I'm juggling a lot—part-time work, helping family, other responsibilities. So studying happened when it happened, not on any set schedule.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or the gamified tasks that got me to log in. I liked those little challenges and badges—they made it feel less boring. Sometimes I'd just log in to see if there was anything new or to check discussion posts. But if nothing felt pressing, I'd put it off for days.
Q4: The gamified elements actually helped make logging in feel more routine for a while. I'd want to check my progress, see if I'd unlocked anything new. It became almost like checking social media—quick and habitual. But that faded when the novelty wore off and stress from other things piled up.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline since I learn best by listening. I'd play them while doing other things, which worked for me. The gamified tasks also kept me engaged because they broke things into smaller, less overwhelming pieces. Discussion posts were okay too—hearing other students' perspectives helped things click sometimes.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and textbook materials. With low resources, I couldn't always access or print everything, and honestly reading just doesn't stick for me the way listening does. I'd skim them last minute if at all. Some of the optional quizzes I skipped entirely because they felt like extra work without real payoff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was interactive or gave me immediate feedback. The gamified stuff was great for that—I could see progress right away. If something felt like busywork or I couldn't see how it connected to the exam, I'd lose interest fast. I needed to feel like it was actually helping me learn, not just ticking boxes.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore, honestly. Required ones had that pressure behind them, so I'd eventually get to them even if I procrastinated. Optional stuff, unless it was fun like the gamified tasks, just fell off my radar. I didn't have the energy to do extra when I was already stretched thin.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download audio lectures and listen to them on my phone when I didn't have good internet. I also recorded myself summarizing concepts and played those back. My notes were handwritten because I couldn't always afford to print things, so I'd study from those extensively.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened when I was walking around listening to downloaded lectures or reviewing my handwritten notes at home. None of that shows up anywhere in the LMS. I'd also have conversations with classmates in person or over the phone about course material, which obviously isn't tracked. The LMS only sees a fraction of what I was doing.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or audio file once and then listen to it multiple times over the next few weeks. It was more practical given my internet situation isn't always reliable. So one download might represent hours of actual study time that the system would never know about.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was dealing with stress from financial stuff and family obligations, plus having nine courses was overwhelming. My motivation just tanked—I felt like I was drowning. Even though I wasn't anxious exactly, I just felt disconnected and couldn't bring myself to care as much.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with family stuff, the LMS was the last thing on my mind. I'd log in less, skip discussion posts, let assignments pile up. The workload from having so many courses made it impossible to give each one proper attention. Something always had to give.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd be logged in, watching a video, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Just going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. Those sessions felt pointless but I'd do them anyway thinking something might stick. It rarely did when I was that checked out.
Q15: I avoided assignments constantly even knowing they mattered. My assignment completion was terrible because I kept telling myself I'd do them later. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed, part was just not being motivated enough to push through the discomfort. The exam I could cram for, but assignments required sustained effort I couldn't muster.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving on assignments. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then panic and try to throw something together. For the LMS, deadlines meant I'd suddenly log in a lot more in the days before something was due. Without deadlines, I'd drift indefinitely.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it, but I often submitted things so late or incomplete that the feedback felt irrelevant. When I did well on the exam, that motivated me briefly. But mostly I felt disconnected from the feedback loop because my engagement was so sporadic to begin with.
Q18: The course structure with gamified elements helped a lot—it made things feel achievable in small chunks. But having so much content across nine courses meant I couldn't really follow any single course's structure properly. I'd jump around based on what seemed most urgent rather than following the intended flow.
Q19: The gamification definitely made engagement easier—it appealed to something in me that wanted those small wins. But the sheer volume of content and the reliance on reading materials made it harder. If more things had been audio-based or interactive, I would've engaged more consistently. The design assumed more time and resources than I had.
Q20: That's literally me. I was in the LMS a lot, watching videos, doing the gamified stuff, checking discussions. But actually completing and submitting assignments? That required a different kind of effort I couldn't sustain. Being present in the system doesn't mean you're producing output—sometimes you're just trying to stay afloat.
Q21: That also makes sense to me because I did well on exams despite barely being in the LMS sometimes. I studied in ways that worked for me—listening, reviewing notes, talking things through. The exam tested what I actually knew, not how often I logged in. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline work, the mental processing, the conversations with peers. They also can't tell if you were genuinely engaged or just had the tab open while doing something else. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it—not just showing up. For me, it's when I'm actively listening, making connections, thinking about how concepts apply. It's mental investment, not just physical presence. You can be engaged without being visible, and visible without being engaged.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through audio and conversations, or how my personal circumstances affected my participation. It wouldn't capture that I actually understood the material well enough to score high on exams despite low assignment completion. My engagement was real, just not in ways the system could measure.
